title: Sign Language Recognition
description: Real-time computer vision system using MediaPipe to interpret hand gestures, arm positioning, and facial expressions, converting them into text.
problemStatement: Build a recognition pipeline that can continuously read expressive sign movements and output structured text with low latency.
designIntent: Create an accessibility-focused interface that can understand combined hand, arm, and facial cues instead of isolated finger shapes.
architecture: Camera frames are captured through OpenCV, landmarks are extracted by MediaPipe, and a gesture-state interpreter maps temporal motion patterns to language tokens.
systemUsed: Python processing pipeline, OpenCV video capture and preprocessing, MediaPipe landmarks, and rule-based gesture interpretation.
stackRationale: Python enabled rapid prototyping and iteration of vision algorithms.
OpenCV provided stable camera stream handling and frame preprocessing.
MediaPipe gave robust real-time body/hand landmark extraction without heavy custom training overhead.
buildProcess: Calibrated camera framing and normalized frame input under different lighting conditions.
Extracted hand, face, and upper-body landmarks and transformed them into consistent feature vectors.
Implemented gesture-state logic to map motion + posture combinations to text tokens.
Built confidence checks and temporal smoothing to reduce jitter in live predictions.
howItWorks: Camera stream is captured frame-by-frame and cleaned before landmark extraction.
Landmark coordinates are analyzed for relative distances, orientation, and movement direction.
The interpretation engine validates gesture context over time, then emits structured text output.
imageInsights: Primary capture frame used to validate landmark quality, gesture boundaries, and real-time output consistency.
challengesAndFixes: Challenge: unstable predictions in fast motion. Fix: temporal smoothing and confidence thresholds.
Challenge: variable lighting. Fix: frame normalization and stabilized detection region configuration.
impact: Demonstrated practical real-time accessibility support while proving strong capability in computer vision system integration.

title: 3D Interactive Anatomy
description: Web-based 3D anatomy explorer with gender toggle and expandable structure navigation.
contributionNote: Designed modular UI flow and 3D interaction behavior.
problemStatement: Create an intuitive browser-based anatomy explorer where non-technical users can inspect complex structures clearly.
designIntent: Prioritize discoverability and orientation in 3D scenes so users can study structures without getting lost.
architecture: React manages interface state and controls, while Three.js renders the anatomy scene and responds to UI-driven selection events.
systemUsed: React component architecture, Three.js scene management, and Tailwind UI system for responsive layout and controls.
stackRationale: React enabled predictable component state for filters, toggles, and anatomy info panels.
Three.js handled real-time 3D rendering and camera interaction efficiently in-browser.
Tailwind accelerated consistent responsive styling for dense interactive controls.
buildProcess: Structured the app into reusable modules for model controls, info panels, and view modes.
Built a model navigation tree for progressive disclosure of body systems.
Implemented smooth camera movement and selection feedback to improve orientation in 3D space.
howItWorks: User selections trigger model visibility and focus updates in the Three.js scene.
UI state controls system filtering, gender switch, and annotated detail overlays.
The explorer preserves context so users can move between regions without losing orientation.
imageInsights: Scene preview demonstrates the layered UI + 3D model integration where users drill down anatomy structures interactively.
challengesAndFixes: Challenge: complex scene navigation for new users. Fix: hierarchical structure panel and focused camera transitions.
Challenge: UI clutter. Fix: progressive disclosure and modular panel toggles.
impact: Turned complex anatomical visualization into a usable learning interface that balances detail and simplicity.

title: Baymax
description: Human-centered emotional tracking system for mood logging and support interaction.
contributionNote: Focused on experience flow and reliable mood-event recording logic.
problemStatement: Design a lightweight emotional check-in assistant that encourages consistent user reflection and support prompts.
designIntent: Keep emotional logging frictionless while preserving enough context for meaningful trend analysis.
architecture: React Native handles mobile interaction and timeline views; Node.js service logic organizes mood events and response trigger rules.
systemUsed: React Native front-end for mobile interaction and Node.js logic for structured mood event processing.
stackRationale: React Native allowed quick cross-platform interface development.
Node.js simplified event-driven processing and lightweight API behavior.
Structured event schemas enabled trend grouping and prompt generation.
buildProcess: Designed mood input patterns that reduce friction and increase daily usage consistency.
Implemented event tagging and timeline grouping for trend visibility.
Defined support interaction rules to trigger contextual responses.
howItWorks: Users submit mood states with optional context and trigger tags.
Entries are grouped into trend views for review and reflection.
Interaction logic surfaces response prompts based on recent emotional patterns.
imageInsights: Interface sample shows the core mood-check-in flow and support response interaction model.
challengesAndFixes: Challenge: user drop-off in repeated logging. Fix: simplified input path and short guided options.
Challenge: noisy trend interpretation. Fix: normalized event tags and grouped timeline logic.
impact: Delivered a clean emotional logging system that demonstrates product thinking plus full-stack implementation discipline.

title: Telegram Bot
description: Automation bot with event-driven command routing and real-time response handling.
contributionNote: Built routing logic, command handlers, and reliable response patterns.
problemStatement: Automate repetitive chat workflows with predictable command behavior and low response latency.
designIntent: Design a resilient command system that remains easy to expand while preserving deterministic responses.
architecture: Incoming Telegram updates are parsed by a command router, mapped to handlers, and executed through modular service functions.
systemUsed: Python command engine and Telegram Bot API webhook/polling event handling.
stackRationale: Python allowed fast handler creation and integration with external utilities.
Telegram API provided clear event contracts for command-driven workflows.
Modular handler design improved maintainability and easier feature extension.
buildProcess: Defined command map and argument parsing strategy.
Implemented handler pipeline with error-safe fallbacks.
Added contextual responses for improved user interaction quality.
howItWorks: Incoming messages are classified into command patterns.
Mapped handlers execute requested actions and compose response payloads.
System returns results in real time and logs invalid command paths for refinement.
imageInsights: Bot preview highlights command-response orchestration and automated interaction behavior.
challengesAndFixes: Challenge: malformed user inputs. Fix: stricter argument parsing and fallback help responses.
Challenge: handler growth complexity. Fix: modular routing table and isolated command modules.
impact: Reduced manual workflow overhead while proving event-driven backend logic and API integration capability.

title: MediTracker
description: Embedded monitoring system with Arduino, housed in a custom 3D-printed enclosure. Designed through iterative prototyping.
contributionNote: Solo hardware and software integration from concept to physical prototype.
problemStatement: Build a portable embedded monitoring platform that is mechanically robust, serviceable, and manufacturable with local prototyping resources.
designIntent: Integrate electronics and enclosure design as one system so physical constraints, serviceability, and firmware requirements align.
architecture: SolidWorks models define enclosure geometry and mounting points; Arduino firmware controls sensing/logic; iterative print-test loops close the hardware validation cycle.
systemUsed: SolidWorks for enclosure design, Arduino firmware in C++, iterative FDM 3D printing for fit/function validation.
stackRationale: SolidWorks provided precise parametric control for enclosure tolerances and internal component alignment.
Arduino offered a practical embedded platform for fast integration and hardware debugging.
3D printing enabled rapid prototype iteration to validate design decisions physically.
buildProcess: Designed initial enclosure and internal mounting strategy in SolidWorks.
Printed test bodies, validated board fit, cable routing, and airflow.
Reworked geometry and tolerances after real assembly feedback.
Finalized firmware + enclosure pair for practical integrated operation.
howItWorks: Sensor and control hardware interface through an Arduino core running custom embedded logic.
Enclosure layout protects electronics while enabling access to key service points.
The final model balances structural protection, usability, and manufacturability.
imageInsights: Initial physical prototype used to validate overall enclosure volume and board fit.
Second iteration showing structural refinement around component access points.
Third revision focused on mounting alignment and cable routing stability.
Fourth revision with improved enclosure ergonomics and assembly practicality.
Expanded design view demonstrating updated modeling decisions before print.
Final SolidWorks drawing (PNG) used to communicate precise geometry before fabrication.
Integrated two-part assembly view highlighting fit between major enclosure segments.
Alternate integrated view confirming final assembly relationship and tolerance intent.
challengesAndFixes: Challenge: board mounting and connector clearance conflicts. Fix: redesigned internal bosses and routing channels.
Challenge: enclosure tolerance mismatch after print. Fix: adjusted CAD offsets and repeated fit-verification cycles.
Challenge: assembly serviceability. Fix: reworked cover split and fastening access geometry.
impact: Demonstrates full-cycle electromechanical execution: CAD modeling, rapid iteration, firmware integration, and physical validation.

title: 3D Printing Projects
description: Custom mechanical components designed and printed for embedded systems, including MediTracker and Smart Miner Helmet.
contributionNote: Owned mechanical design, print preparation, and assembly-fit optimization.
problemStatement: Produce functional mechanical parts that align with electronics constraints, assembly realities, and rapid iteration timelines.
designIntent: Reduce prototype turnaround time while maintaining mechanical integrity and fit quality for embedded applications.
architecture: CAD models are parameterized, exported for slicing, printed for fit validation, then revised based on tolerance and assembly results.
systemUsed: SolidWorks/Fusion 360 CAD workflow with slicer tuning for wall thickness, supports, and print reliability.
stackRationale: SolidWorks and Fusion 360 supported both structured engineering geometry and quick concept variants.
PrusaSlicer tuning enabled repeatable prints with better strength-to-weight balance.
Iterative prototyping allowed rapid correction of mechanical-electrical integration issues.
buildProcess: Modeled components around sensor placement and board envelope constraints.
Tuned print settings for strength-to-weight balance and repeatability.
Validated each revision by physical fit checks and integration tests.
howItWorks: CAD models are exported into print-ready formats with tuned slicing parameters.
Parts are fabricated, tested with target systems, and revised based on tolerance/performance data.
Final versions are selected for integration into embedded prototypes.
imageInsights: Printed component variant used for early form and fit exploration.
Revised geometry with manufacturing-oriented adjustments.
Functional print focused on assembly compatibility.
Finalized part version selected for practical integration.
challengesAndFixes: Challenge: weak sections in load-bearing zones. Fix: increased local wall thickness and adjusted infill strategy.
Challenge: mating-part tolerance drift. Fix: calibration parts and iterative dimension correction.
impact: Enabled faster hardware iteration loops and stronger integration quality across multiple embedded builds.

title: CityLight IQ (LabVIEW)
description: LabVIEW simulation with block diagram architecture, ambient light modeling, and adaptive LED brightness scaling.
problemStatement: Simulate adaptive street-light behavior that responds intelligently to environmental light changes.
designIntent: Model a responsive lighting controller where behavior is transparent, testable, and easy to tune.
architecture: Ambient input simulation feeds event-driven LabVIEW logic that maps conditions to discrete brightness output states.
systemUsed: LabVIEW block-diagram programming with sensor-input simulation and LED output logic.
stackRationale: LabVIEW provided clear visual control-flow representation for rapid simulation iteration.
Event structures enabled reactive behavior modeling for changing environmental input.
Block-diagram approach made logic validation and tuning straightforward.
buildProcess: Defined operating states and threshold rules for LED scaling.
Implemented event-driven control structure in LabVIEW blocks.
Validated transitions for smooth brightness adaptation behavior.
howItWorks: Simulated ambient input is sampled and compared against calibrated thresholds.
Control logic selects one of multiple brightness states for LED outputs.
Event-handling structure updates outputs dynamically as conditions change.
challengesAndFixes: Challenge: unstable threshold switching. Fix: tuned transition conditions and hysteresis behavior.
Challenge: readability of control logic at scale. Fix: modularized block sections by function.
impact: Showcases control-logic thinking and simulation-first validation for smart infrastructure use cases.

title: Magnetic Levitation System
description: Dynamic modeling and control system in MATLAB & Simulink.
contributionNote: Group project - Designed compensator and implemented control model in Simulink.
problemStatement: Stabilize an inherently unstable magnetic levitation plant through model-based control design.
designIntent: Achieve stable levitation with acceptable transient behavior while respecting system response constraints.
architecture: Plant dynamics are modeled in MATLAB and closed-loop behavior is implemented in Simulink with a compensator shaping loop response.
systemUsed: MATLAB system analysis and Simulink closed-loop model implementation.
stackRationale: MATLAB offered robust control analysis tools for pole/response characterization.
Simulink enabled clear visual validation of closed-loop system behavior over time.
Combined workflow accelerated compensator tuning and verification cycles.
buildProcess: Developed linearized plant representation and response analysis.
Designed compensator to satisfy stability and transient-response requirements.
Implemented and tested closed-loop behavior in Simulink.
howItWorks: Plant model receives setpoint and disturbance conditions.
Compensator shapes loop dynamics for stable levitation response.
Simulink validates settling behavior, overshoot limits, and robustness trends.
challengesAndFixes: Challenge: oscillatory response. Fix: compensator retuning and stability-margin checks.
Challenge: balancing speed vs stability. Fix: iterative parameter sweeps in Simulink.
impact: Demonstrates control-systems competence and practical contribution in collaborative engineering projects.

title: Fountain Nozzle
description: Mechanical nozzle design study with multiple output concepts modeled in SolidWorks and compared for manufacturability and flow behavior intent.
contributionNote: Concept-to-CAD workflow with variant design exploration.
problemStatement: Develop nozzle concepts with different spray behavior objectives while maintaining practical geometry for production-ready mechanical modeling.
designIntent: Convert conceptual fountain ideas into structured CAD variants that can be analyzed for geometry quality, assembly logic, and manufacturability.
architecture: Concept reference is translated into part features, then assembled into nozzle variants to compare directional behavior and integration constraints.
systemUsed: SolidWorks part and assembly modeling workflow with iterative dimensional refinement.
stackRationale: SolidWorks provided strong assembly tools for multi-part nozzle configuration studies.
Parametric modeling enabled rapid variation of outlet geometry and internal paths.
Iterative CAD allowed controlled exploration of form/function tradeoffs.
buildProcess: Image 1 captures initial concept inspiration and geometric intent.
Converted concept into baseline nozzle architecture in SolidWorks.
Designed multiple variants to compare directional flow behavior and structural form.
Prepared assembly-focused models for downstream fabrication readiness.
howItWorks: Each nozzle geometry changes outlet path and therefore expected jet pattern behavior.
Assembly models define alignment and integration constraints for supporting components.
Design variants are evaluated for feasibility, complexity, and target visual/flow objective.
imageInsights: Image 1 records the original inspiration concept that guided geometry direction.
Assembly-dagi model demonstrates one nozzle architecture and its structural logic.
Assembly-multi model presents a multi-output configuration for broader spray behavior.
Straight-jet nozzle model focuses on directed flow with cleaner outlet geometry.
challengesAndFixes: Challenge: preserving concept aesthetics while keeping model manufacturable. Fix: balanced feature simplification with key visual intent.
Challenge: variant comparison complexity. Fix: standardized parameter baselines across design versions.
impact: Shows CAD depth, concept translation skill, and mechanical variant reasoning for product-oriented design work.
